serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried anise seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.1 g), used as a spice or flavouring in cooking, baking, or herbal teas.

nutrient_source.title: Spices, anise seed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170921)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for dried anise seeds (Pimpinella anisum) used as primary nutrient reference. Core macros and micronutrients cross-referenced with Singapore HPB database values provided in input record; HPB values retained where consistent. Minor discrepancies noted in iron (HPB: 36.96 mg vs USDA: 36.96 mg — consistent). Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol values are consistent between HPB and USDA sources and are retained from HPB input. Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170921.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices and seeds — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for whole seeds and legumes with comparable carbohydrate and fibre profiles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried anise seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on the following rationale: (1) anise seeds have a very high dietary fibre content of 14.6 g/100g, which substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (2) the carbohydrate fraction includes a large proportion of complex polysaccharides; (3) analogous whole spice seeds with high fibre content (e.g., fenugreek seeds, GI ~25 per University of Sydney GI Database) are used as the closest defensible comparator. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (25 × 1.05) / 100 = 0.26, where carbohydrate per 2.1 g serving = 50.02 × 0.021 = 1.05 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (1441 mg/100g) with high dietary fibre (14.6 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Dried anise seeds are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; their outstanding sodium-to-potassium ratio (1:90) strongly supports DASH dietary targets, and regular use as a culinary spice contributes to blood pressure reduction through potassium-mediated vasodilation and fibre-driven improvements in vascular function.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high dietary fibre (14.6 g/100g), negligible sugar, and bioactive anethole compounds with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried anise seeds are appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the high fibre content slows glucose absorption and reduces postprandial glycaemic excursions, while anethole and related phenylpropanoids have demonstrated insulin-sensitising and glucose-lowering effects in preclinical studies — patients on antidiabetic medication should monitor for additive hypoglycaemic effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.59 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (1441 mg/100g), high dietary fibre (14.6 g/100g), and anti-inflammatory polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried anise seeds present a favourable cardiovascular profile with negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high fibre that supports LDL reduction; their rich polyphenol content (anethole, flavonoids) provides anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity relevant to atherosclerosis prevention, and their high potassium content supports cardiac rhythm and blood pressure regulation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (anethole, flavonoids), very low saturated fat, high fibre supporting gut immune modulation, and high calcium (646 mg/100g) for bone protection
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried anise seeds are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; their concentrated anti-inflammatory phytochemicals, particularly anethole, may help modulate inflammatory pathways, while high calcium content supports bone density protection — culinary use as a spice is safe, but concentrated anise seed oil should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried anise seeds (Pimpinella anisum) are the small, oval, grey-brown seeds of the anise plant, a flowering herb in the Apiaceae family native to the eastern Mediterranean and Southwest Asia. The seeds are harvested at maturity and dried, yielding a firm, ridged seed approximately 3–5 mm in length with a characteristic sweet, warm, licorice-like aroma and flavour derived primarily from the volatile compound trans-anethole (80–90% of essential oil). Dried anise seeds are used extensively as a culinary spice in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and European cuisines — in bread, pastries, sausages, liqueurs (e.g., ouzo, arak, pastis), herbal teas, and savoury dishes. They are distinct from star anise (Illicium verum), which shares a similar flavour profile but belongs to a different botanical family. Nutritionally, dried anise seeds are exceptionally dense: per 100 g they provide 337 kcal, 17.6 g protein, 15.9 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated), 50.02 g carbohydrate, and 14.6 g dietary fibre — making them one of the highest-fibre spices available. They are extraordinarily rich in potassium (1441 mg/100g), calcium (646 mg/100g), and iron (36.96 mg/100g), and are very low in sodium (16 mg/100g). Clinically, their high fibre and anethole content support glycaemic control, blood pressure management, and cardiovascular health. They are plant-based, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Typical culinary use is 1–2 teaspoons (2–4 g) per dish, so absolute nutrient contribution per serving is modest but cumulative with regular use.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB database (input confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170921) for dried anise seeds (Pimpinella anisum). Values are consistent between both approved sources. Additional micronutrients (saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, vitamins, minerals) sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using fenugreek seeds as the closest defensible comparator, documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170921 for dried anise seeds; this is confirmed zero per approved source — the carbohydrate fraction is dominated by complex polysaccharides and dietary fibre rather than free sugars. 2. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; anise seeds are a plant-derived spice with no trans fatty acids. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; these are plant-derived seeds with no animal-derived nutrient content. 4. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: detailed tocopherol fractionation data for dried anise seeds not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 25, low) based on fenugreek seeds from the University of Sydney GI Database as the closest defensible comparator; no direct GI measurement for Pimpinella anisum seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA; anise seeds are a plant food with no cholesterol. 7. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag in the input has been removed as anise seeds are spice seeds, not cereal grains; all other dietary tags (plant_based, low_sodium, high_protein, high_fibre) are retained as accurate. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT code 227318004 are cited as defensible matches based on published ontology documentation; FoodEx2 code A00LC is cited as the closest defensible match for dried anise seeds in the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system.
